coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots-based fluorescent nanoprobe for ultra-sensitive detection of travoprost acid in aqueous humor: Greenness and blueness assessment with intensive pharmacokinetic study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdel-Hafez, Noha G.
Ali, Marwa F.B.
El-Gizawy, Samia M.
Atia, Noha N.
coredata.dc:description: Travoprost (TRV) is an efficient prostaglandin (PG) analogue, which is hydrolyzed by corneal esterases to its active acid form (TRV acid) after absorption. Till now no spectrofluorimetric methods have been reported for detection of TRV acid in biological fluids. Due to high sensitivity, simplicity and rapidity; a novel and ultra-sensitive spectrofluorometric method with an efficient solid phase extraction (SPE) technique was developed for determination of TRV acid in aqueous humor (AH). The study relies on the fluorescence enhancement of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), which were prepared via a hydrothermal treatment of triphenylamine and citric acid. The formed nanoprobe responds with very high sensitivity to trace amounts of TRV acid through hydrogen bonding formation, which is accompanied by an enhancement in fluorescence intensity due to N-CDs aggregation. The proposed method showed excellent sensitivity for analyzing TRV acid in the range of 1–200 ng/mL, with a very low limit of detection (0.29 ng/mL). Additionally, the method’s selectivity for analyzing TRV acid in the presence of common ions and anticipated co-administered drugs was assessed. The structural and chemical properties of the prepared N-CDs were extensively characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Furthermore, the method’s applicability was evaluated by studying the pharmacokinetic properties of TRV acid in AH. The environmental impact of the method was evaluated using various greenness assessment tools, confirming the eco-friendly nature of the proposed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Travoprost acid
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence detection
Pharmacokinetic study
Aqueous humor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for spectrofluorometric determination of TRV acid
Morphological and spectroscopic characterization of N-CDs
Comparison of the proposed method with the previously published ones

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM of N-CDs (Inset is HRTEM), (B) TEM of N-CDs after addition of the drug, (C) XRD of N-CDs, and (D) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs alone and after addition of the drug, (E) UV–visible absorption (black), fluorescence excitation (red), and emission (blue) spectra of the N-CDs. The inset shows photographs taken under ambient light and 365 nm UV illumination, and (F) Emission spectra of the N-CDs at excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 370 nm.

(A) The effect of eight concentration levels of TRV acid (1–200 ng/mL) on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, (B) Relationship of (ΔF) against concentration of TRV acid, (C) The selectivity study of the effect of N-CDs towards TRV acid in presence of anticipated species, and (D) Optimization of the eluting solvent composition used in SPE.
(A) Proposed sensing mechanism of TRV acid with N-CDs, and (B) TRV acid concentration–time profiles (mean ± SD) in AH of 6 rabbits following ocular instillation of 1 drop of Travonorm® eye drop.
Statistical data for fluorometric determination of TRV acid using the prepared N-CDs.
Assay of TRV acid in spiked AH.
Evaluation of the greenness and blueness of the proposed fluorometric method.
Comparison of the proposed spectrofluorimetric method with the reported methods.

row.entry: LLE with a mixture of ethyl acetate: n −hexane followed by evaporation and reconstitution
Centrifugation, SPE followed by evaporation and reconstitution

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots-based fluorescent nanoprobe for ultra-sensitive detection of travoprost acid in aqueous humor: Greenness and blueness assessment with intensive pharmacokinetic study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut 71526, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut 71526, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Travoprost (TRV) is an efficient prostaglandin (PG) analogue, which is hydrolyzed by corneal esterases to its active acid form (TRV acid) after absorption. Till now no spectrofluorimetric methods have been reported for detection of TRV acid in biological fluids. Due to high sensitivity, simplicity and rapidity; a novel and ultra-sensitive spectrofluorometric method with an efficient solid phase extraction (SPE) technique was developed for determination of TRV acid in aqueous humor (AH). The study relies on the fluorescence enhancement of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), which were prepared via a hydrothermal treatment of triphenylamine and citric acid. The formed nanoprobe responds with very high sensitivity to trace amounts of TRV acid through hydrogen bonding formation, which is accompanied by an enhancement in fluorescence intensity due to N-CDs aggregation. The proposed method showed excellent sensitivity for analyzing TRV acid in the range of 1–200 ng/mL, with a very low limit of detection (0.29 ng/mL). Additionally, the method’s selectivity for analyzing TRV acid in the presence of common ions and anticipated co-administered drugs was assessed. The structural and chemical properties of the prepared N-CDs were extensively characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Furthermore, the method’s applicability was evaluated by studying the pharmacokinetic properties of TRV acid in AH. The environmental impact of the method was evaluated using various greenness assessment tools, confirming the eco-friendly nature of the proposed method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glaucoma is a prevalent eye disease that can result in irreversible blindness if left undiagnosed and untreated. Axonal degeneration in the optic nerve and the progressive loss of retinal ganglion cells may contribute to the progression of visual field damage. To date, there has been no medication or surgical intervention that can stop or reverse damage to the optic nerve; however, treatment strategies focus on delaying disease progression by controlling the intraocular pressure (IOP) [1–3]. Prostaglandin (PG) receptor analogues are the first line treatment for glaucoma [4]. These agents are formulated as ester prodrugs to enhance their lipophilicity, thereby improving their corneal permeability [5]. Upon absorption, they are hydrolyzed by corneal esterases into their active acid forms [6]. PG analogues selectively bind to PGF2α receptors in the ciliary muscle, promoting relaxation and increasing aqueous humor (AH) outflow via both the trabecular meshwork and the uveoscleral pathways [7,8]. Topical PG analogs rarely cause systemic side effects. However, when they do occur, these side effects can be very serious, ranging from dizziness and muscle/joint pain to hypertensive crisis [9,10]. The elevation in blood pressure is due to activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system [11]. Additionally, topical PG analogs can exacerbate asthma and lead to rhinitis [10].

Travoprost (TRV) (Fig. S1A), a key member of this class, is metabolized in the eye to yield its active form, TRV acid (Fig. S1B). TRV, like other antiglaucoma drugs, is applied for several months or years; this necessitates the establishment of sensitive and accurate analytical methods for determination of its active form in different biological fluids, thereby aiding in the understanding of its pharmacokinetics and avoiding the occurrence of any systemic side effects. Additionally, the concentration of antiglaucoma drugs in biological fluids is very low, necessitating efficient extraction techniques to enrich the sample and eliminate the matrix effect. The analysis of TRV acid in real sample matrices has been conducted only using LC/MS/MS [12–14]. Despite the sensitivity of these methods, they require tedious pretreatment steps and consume significant volumes of organic solvents, along with the complexity and expense of tandem mass spectrometry. Therefore, there is a need to establish new sensors for the efficient and sensitive estimation of TRV acid.

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanosphere composites with a maximum particle size of 10 nm [15]. Since their emergence in 2004, CDs have attracted significant interest because of their distinctive features, such as biocompatibility, high photoluminescence, chemical stability, and cost-effective synthesis [16]. These attributes render them excellent candidates for various sensing applications, especially in the field of biological analysis. CDs have been widely employed in the detection of biologically relevant molecules and processes, including the monitoring of enzymatic activity, the detection of disease biomarkers, and the quantification of certain metabolites such as glucose, uric acid, and cholesterol [17–19].

Doping of CDs with heteroatoms such as boron (B), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S) can enhance the optical and surface modification of CDs [20–22]. The nitrogen (N) atom is widely used as a dopant due to its similarity to carbon (C) atom in the form of atomic radius [23]. The as-synthesized nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) displayed some advantages such as excellent water solubility, high stability, very good sensitivity, and satisfactory selectivity towards TRV acid detection. This study relies on the fluorescence enhancement of N-CDs. Fluorescence turn-on is preferred over turn-off because it reduces the likelihood of false positive results caused by other fluorescent quenchers present in samples [24]. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was enhanced by TRV acid, which is believed to result from the formation of strong hydrogen bonds between the drug and N-CDs.

Generally, spectrofluorimetric methods are known for their high sensitivity, simplicity, and rapidity which expand their range of applications. Therefore, this work presents the first spectrofluorimetric method for the analysis of TRV acid in rabbit AH and studying its pharmacokinetic properties. It is important to note that this is the first reported use of fluorescent N-CDs for the detection of TRV acid in real sample matrices. Although the concentration of proteins in AH is significantly lower (<1 %) than that found in plasma [25], a highly effective solid-phase extraction (SPE) technique has been established to effectively eliminate the matrix effect associated with such measurements in the proposed study. The represented study was applied for studying various pharmacokinetic parameters of TRV acid. The environmental impact of the method was further evaluated using various greenness assessment tools, demonstrating that it is eco-friendly. Additionally, the practicality of the method was assessed using the Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI).

Travoprost acid (>98.0 %) was purchased from Biosynth Carbosynth (Berkshire, UK). Travonorm® eye drops (0.004 %) were purchased from local pharmacies. Triphenylamine with a purity of 98 % and citric acid monohydrate (99 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Methanol (>98 %), NaCl (98 %), hydrogen peroxide (30 %), and sodium hydroxide (>98 %) were obtained from Fluka (Buchs, Switzerland). The buffer used was Britton Robinson Buffer (BR). Double distilled water was used for preparation of all aqueous solutions.
Refer to supplementary material.

N-CDs were synthesized as previously mentioned [26], with slight modifications. Fig. 1 illustrates the steps of synthesis of N-CDs. Briefly, a solution was prepared by dissolving 3 g each of citric acid monohydrate and triphenylamine in 25 mL of a 1:1 (v/v) water–ethanol mixture. The solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and subjected to thermal treatment at 200 °C for 9 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min, followed by filtration through a 0.22 µm membrane filter. For additional purification and to eliminate any impurities, the solution underwent dialysis using a dialysis bag 1000 Da for 2 days. Freeze-drying was used to obtain the N-CDs.

To prepare a stock solution of TRV acid (100 µg/mL), 10 mg of TRV acid was dissolved in 100 mL of methanol, with the aid of sonication for 10 min. Then the solution was stored in the refrigerator. A stock solution of the prepared N-CDs (100 µg/mL) was obtained by addition of 10 mg N-CDs to a 100 mL volumetric flask, then the volume was completed to the mark with double distilled water and stored in the refrigerator (4 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for spectrofluorometric determination of TRV acid
ce:section.ce:para: A solution was prepared by combining 200 μL of the synthesized N-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) with 1 mL of TRV acid solution at varying concentrations, and the final volume was adjusted to 10 mL with BR buffer (pH 11). Fluorescence spectra were recorded in the 260–550 nm range, with excitation wavelength at 310 nm.
Refer to supplementary material.

The SPE procedure for TRV acid from AH was conducted as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The rabbit AH sample (200 μL) was spiked with 200 μL of different concentrations of TRV acid solution within the range (1–200 ng/mL) into 1.5 mL Eppendorf's tubes then completed to 800 μL with double distilled water. The SPE procedure was carried out in four steps: conditioning with 1 mL of methanol, followed by 1 mL of double distilled water; sample loading; washing with 1 mL of 5 % methanol and then 1 mL of double distilled water; and finally, the elution step with a mixture of methanol and BR at pH 11 (80: 20, v/v). The obtained solution was processed as mentioned in Section 2.5, and a blank free from the drug was carried out in all the measurements.

A pharmacokinetic study was performed on six healthy New Zealand rabbits (both male and female), each weighing between 2.0 and 2.5 kg. The rabbits had free access to water and diet and were housed under standard laboratory conditions, which included alternate night and day cycles of 12 h each, a constant temperature (25 °C ± 4), and relative humidity (55–65 %). The study protocol received approval from the Institutional Review Board and the research ethics committee of the Faculty of Medicine at Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt (IRB approval number 04-2023-200631). One drop of Travonorm® eye drops (∼50 μL of 0.004 % solution) were instilled into the cul-de-sac of both eyes. AH was collected and treated as previously mentioned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and spectroscopic characterization of N-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology and size distribution of the synthesized N-CDs were comprehensively analyzed using TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the N-CDs exhibited a uniform spherical geometry with a mean particle diameter of 3.4 nm. The particle size distribution, illustrated as a histogram in Fig. 3A, further corroborates the well-dispersed nature of the nanospheres. The inset of Fig. 3A shows the High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of N-CDs with lattice fringes of 0.38 nm, this value is slightly larger than the typical d-spacing of pure graphite (0.34 nm), and this increase is attributed to the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the carbon framework, which induces structural distortion and expands the graphitic layers [27]. Fig. 3B shows the aggregated spheres upon interaction with TRV acid, which may be due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the drug and the synthesized N-CDs [26].

The XRD pattern (Fig. 3C) exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered around 26°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. This broadness and the low intensity of the peak indicate a low degree of crystallinity and the presence of disordered graphitic structures, which is a typical feature of carbon-based nanomaterials doped with heteroatoms such as nitrogen [27].

FT-IR analysis of N-CDs was conducted before and after the addition of TRV acid to identify the surface functionality of the prepared N-CDs and to explain the proposed sensing mechanism. From Fig. 3D, the spectrum of N-CDs shows a broad band at 3435 cm−1, which is typically assigned to OH/N–H stretching vibration. Furthermore, a sharp band at 2957 cm−1 was observed, assigned to C–H stretching vibration. A strong and sharp peak at 1735 cm−1 is characteristic of carbonyl group stretching vibration. The peak that appeared at 1592 cm−1 may indicate C=C stretching in alkenes or aromatic systems [28]. The strong peak at 1408 cm−1 often corresponds to bending vibrations (e.g., C–H bending) in aliphatic groups. The strong peak at 1203 cm−1 may represent C–O stretching in alcohols or possibly C–N stretching in amines. A minor peak at 767 cm−1 is often indicative of C–H bending in aromatic compounds, suggesting the presence of a substituted benzene ring [29]. The spectrum suggests that the prepared N-CDs may contain alcohols, carbonyls, carboxylic groups, and possibly aromatic or unsaturated systems.

The FT-IR spectrum of the N-CDs was also conducted after the addition of the drug (Fig. 3D). Multiple changes upon the addition of TRV acid suggest specific interactions between the drug and N-CDs. The broadening and shift of the hydroxyl peak at 3384 cm−1 suggest an increased hydrogen bonding or interactions involving hydroxyl groups in TRV acid and the N-CDs [30,31]. The very strong peak at 2926 cm−1 may indicate enhanced C–H stretching, which may result from the interaction of the drug with the N-CDs, possibly indicating a conformational change or a more organized structure in the presence of the drug. The decrease in the intensity of the peak at 1735 cm−1 suggests that the carbonyl group might be participated in the interactions with the carbon dots, potentially forming hydrogen bonds or undergoing some steric hindrance. The peaks at 1456 cm−1 and 1104 cm−1 may correspond to C–H bending and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively indicating stronger interactions, and possibly these peaks can be attributed to TRV acid [32].
The chemical composition of the prepared N-CDs was also studied using XPS analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. S2. For more information refer to supplementary material.

Fig. 3E shows two recognizable absorption bands of N-CDs in UV–vis region, located at 238 and 280 nm. These peaks correspond to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. The first band indicates the presence of C=C bonds, while the second band suggests the presence of C=O or C=N moieties [33]. The N-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence emission at 410 nm following excitation at 310 nm, as depicted in Fig. 3F. Furthermore, different excitation wavelengths (300–370 nm) were used to record the emission spectra, confirming that the fluorescence intensity of the prepared N-CDs is dependent on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 3F).
Refer to supplementary material.

The stability of N-CDs as fluorescent probes was established by studying the degree of change in fluorescence intensity upon exposure of N-CDs to various factors. Fig. S3A demonstrates the fluorescence performance of N-CDs at different pH values (2–12) and indicates that the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs increased by increasing pH value from 2 and reaching its maximum intensity at pH 8, before declining as the pH increased after pH 8. The effect of different concentrations of NaCl (0.01–2 mol/L) on the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs is illustrated in Fig. S3B, confirming the relative stability of N-CDs even at high concentration of NaCl. Additionally, the N-CDs were exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp for up to 3 hrs. Fig. S3C confirms the photostability of the prepared N-CDs. Furthermore, the effect of oxidizing agents, such as H2O2, on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is shown in Fig. S3D, which confirms that the fluorescence intensity was minimally affected by exposure to different concentrations (2–10 mmol/L) of H2O2. The stability of the N-CDs upon exposure to high temperatures (35–115 °C) was also studied and illustrated in Fig. S3E, confirming their excellent stability over a wider temperature range.

Different parameters were optimized to attain the highest fluorescence intensity. The interaction between N-CDs and TRV acid was studied at different pH values (2–12) to determine the optimum pH. As depicted in Fig. S4A, the maximum fluorescence intensity was attained at pH 11. Even though the N-CDs fluorescence intensity was moderately low at this pH (Fig. S3A), it produced the highest fluorescence intensity with TRV acid. These findings indicate that the interaction between TRV acid and N-CDs is pH dependent, resulting in fluorescence enhancement [27]. This enhancement in the fluorescence intensity could be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds, which, in turn, changed the surface characteristics of N-CDs at different pH levels. Other parameters, such as the volume of added N-CDs (Fig. S4B), incubation time (Fig. S4C), and the order of addition of the components (Fig. S4D), were optimized. It was concluded that 200 µL of the prepared N-CDs stock solution produced the maximum fluorescence intensity. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity didn’t change by changing the reaction times, so all measurements were accomplished after 1 min. Finally, changing the order of addition of each component-drug, N-CDs, or buffer- didn’t result in any change in fluorescence intensity.

As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence signal of N-CDs was increased with the concentration of TRV acid. Eight concentration levels of TRV acid (Fig. 4B) were used to express the method’s linearity according to the following equation:
(1)
Δ F =
2
. 0
3
TRV acid
+
3
.
69
R
2
= 0 . 9982 ,
n
=
8
ΔF = F − F0 where, F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensities of the sensor with and without TRV acid, respectively. Detection (LOD) and quantitation (LOQ) limits are expressed as 3.3σ /S and 10σ /S, respectively, where σ is the intercept standard deviation and S is the slope. These parameters are summarized in Table 1 .

The accuracy of the analytical method is an important validation parameter; therefore, the fluorescence intensity of three concentration levels (10, 100, and 200 ng/mL) of TRV acid with the prepared N-CDs were measured six times each, and the % recoveries ranged from 97.86 to 101.08 %, as represented in Table S1.

To determine the intra- and inter-day precision of the method, three concentration levels were analyzed six times in the same day and over 3 successive days, respectively. The percentage recoveries and RSD% were calculated, as shown in Table S1. The results explain the excellent precision of the proposed method.

Another important validation parameter is the method’s robustness. The analytical method is deemed robust when small variations in the analytical parameters don’t result in considerable changes in the drug recovery. Minor changes to some experimental parameters, such as, medium pH, excitation wavelength, and volume of N-CDs, were applied, and the % recoveries ranged from 96.14 to 100.6 %, confirming the method’s robustness. These outcomes are represented in Table S2.

Several ions as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe2+, and Zn2+, as well as different species like glucose, ascorbic acid, and uric acid, along with some possible co-administered antiglaucoma drugs such as timolol (TIM), betaxolol (BET), levobunolol (LEV), dorzolamide (DOR), brinzolamide (BRZ), and brimonidine (BRIM), can co-exist and potentially interfere with the analysis of the studied drug if they affect the fluorescence signal of N-CDs. Additionally, benzalkonium chloride, a commonly used preservative, is studied to assess its impact on the N-CDs’ fluorescence signal. The concentration of the anticipated interferences and co-administered drugs was 100 and 40 ng/mL, respectively. The results illustrated in Fig. 4C confirmed that the fluorescence signal of N-CDs wasn’t affected in the presence of these species, demonstrating the high selectivity of the prepared probe for the analysis of TRV acid. The proposed spectrofluorometric method is not selective for travoprost acid alone, as both travoprost and other PG analogues such as tafluprost acid exhibited similar fluorescence enhancement of the N-CDs. This indicates that the method detects a class effect likely related to the structural similarity and functional groups common among PG analogues. Although the proposed method shows similar fluorescence enhancement in the presence of travoprost, travoprost acid, and tafluprost acid, these drugs are not co-administered in clinical practice. Therefore, analytical selectivity between these PG analogues is not required for the intended application of this method. The method is intended for the quantitative determination of TRV acid in a given sample, where no interference from other PG analogues is expected.

AH is a water-like fluid that fills the anterior chamber of the eye, with a protein concentration of <1 % of that present in plasma [25]. However, it is important to establish a very efficient extraction technique to eliminate the false positive effects of proteins in the fluorometric analysis of TRV acid. Several parameters that may influence SPE efficiency, including the washing and eluting solvents as well as the buffer pH, were investigated. The best washing solvents include washing with 1 mL of 5 % methanol followed by 1 mL of double distilled water. The combination that exhibited the highest extraction efficiency was Methanol: BR buffer (pH 11) (80: 20 %), as illustrated in Fig. 4D. The extraction recovery of TRV acid from AH using the proposed SPE method ranged from 95.49 to 97.00 %.

To prove the applicability of the prepared sensor for the determination of TRV acid in biological matrices, the responses of N-CDs toward TRV acid in AH samples were measured. In spiked AH samples, the recoveries obtained ranged from 95.49 to 97.00 %. These results imply that the AH matrix introduces almost no interference with the analysis of TRV acid using the suggested method (Table 2 ).

In accordance with the characterization data, N-CDs contain multiple functional groups (Fig. 5 A) including amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups. These groups can form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl and carboxylate groups present in the TRV acid structure. Upon addition of TRV acid, hydrogen bonding occurs between the functional groups of N-CDs (–OH, –COOH, –NH2) and those on TRV acid. This interaction leads to surface passivation by reducing surface defects and suppressing non-radiative recombination sites. Consequently, radiative recombination efficiency increases, resulting in enhanced fluorescence, as supported by previous studies [34–36]. Furthermore, the addition of TRV acid induces aggregation of N-CDs (Fig. 3B), indicating strong intermolecular interactions. This aggregation likely restricts the rotational and vibrational motion of surface groups, further suppressing non-radiative decay, as demonstrated in similar reports [37–39]. FT-IR analysis (Fig. 3D) supports the involvement of hydrogen bonding. A broad band near 3350 cm−1 corresponds to hydrogen-bonded –OH groups, and the decreased intensity of the carbonyl absorption at 1735 cm−1 indicates participation of the carbonyl group in hydrogen bonding.

TRV acid levels in rabbit AH were measured for up to 6 h using this ultra-sensitive spectrofluorometric technique, paired with the rapid and efficient SPE method. The concentration–time profiles were determined from the observed TRV acid concentrations in the rabbits' AH, with the average concentration–time curves (± SD) shown in Fig. 5B. These profiles were fitted to a two-compartment pharmacokinetic model [14]. The pharmacokinetic parameters in AH are presented as means (± SD) in Table S3. The Tmax of 1.18 h indicates that the drug has a relatively fast absorption rate and a quick onset of action. A very good tissue penetration and a rapid distribution phase were confirmed by the large volume of distribution (V/F = 49.68 (ng)/(ng/ml)). The short half-life of the distribution phase (t1/2Alpha = 0.61 h) supports the idea of rapid redistribution to peripheral tissues. In contrast, the elimination phase (t1/2Beta = 2.68 h) is more prolonged, suggesting that the drug remains in the system for a longer period and is eliminated more slowly, which supports once- or twice-daily dosing [40]. The clearance (CL/F = 20.96 ng/(ng/mL)/h) is moderate, indicating efficient removal without being excessively fast, which allows the drug to maintain therapeutic levels for a reasonable duration.

The principles of green chemistry are widely applied in several areas. Therefore, multiple greenness assessment tools are emerging to determine the environmental impact of different analytical procedures. Recently, different metrics-the Analytical GREEnness calculator (AGREE), the AGREEprep and ComplexMoGAPI-[41–43] have been extensively used for this purpose.

The criteria of AGREE are based on the 12 principles of green analytical chemistry. Twelve variables assigned a value 0–1 each. The total assessment of these variables is presented in the center of a circular shape (Table 3 ). Additionally, the color of the center indicates the method’s greenness. AGREE is a thorough, flexible, and user-friendly evaluation method that yields clear and informative results.

In 2022, a new greenness assessment tool called AGREEprep was established as a modification to the AGREE tool. The AGREEprep covers the sample preparation stage so gives comprehensive information about the ecofriendly nature of the whole method.

Another important approach is the ComplexMoGAPI, which emerged as a modification of GAPI and ComplexGAPI. ComplexMoGAPI is a tool that provides information about the analytical method at all stages, from collection to final analysis. Additionally, it evaluates procedures performed before the general analysis. Finally, it generates a cumulative score that evaluates the overall environmental suitability of the method.
Table 3 illustrates the pictograms of the different greenness tools, and results confirm the eco-friendly nature of the recommended analytical method.

The method’s practicality and performance can be estimated using the BAGI tool [44]. This tool involves four scores, each attributed to a different color: dark blue, blue, light blue, and white, with scores of 10, 7.5, 5, and 2.5, respectively. Dark blue indicates excellent performance, while white indicates no performance. The resulting pictogram contains a number in its center, ranging between 25 and 100, with 25 implying poor practicality and 100 implying excellent practicality. A method is considered applicable if its final score is at least 60. The BAGI score of the current work is 62.5 (Table 3), which confirms its good applicability. Table S4 illustrates the 10 attributes of the BAGI method used for evaluation of the practicality of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed method with the previously published ones
ce:section.ce:para: Very few analytical methods have been reported for the determination of TRV acid in different biological fluids (Table 4 ). The reported methods used HPLC techniques coupled with tandem mass spectrometry. HPLC methods are characterized by their tedious and time-consuming procedures, along with consumption of significant amounts of organic solvents. Additionally, these methods are very expensive and require advanced expertise. A comparison of the proposed spectrofluorometric method with the previously reported methods in terms of technique used, sample preparation, matrix, and limits of quantitation is presented in Table 4. Compared to the reported methods, our study provides several advantages, including simplicity, minimal sample preparation, speed, and cost-effectiveness.

A novel, highly sensitive spectrofluorometric method was developed for the determination of TRV acid in rabbit aqueous humor (AH). The method is based on the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) through the thermal treatment of triphenylamine and citric acid. The method demonstrates exceptional sensitivity, with low detection and quantitation limits (0.29 and 0.87 ng/mL, respectively), making it a valuable tool for pharmacokinetic studies of TRV acid in AH. Characterization of the N-CDs reveals their robust performance and remarkable selectivity in complex biological matrices, ensuring reliable analysis even in the presence of common ions and co-administered drugs. Stability assessments showed that the N-CDs retain their integrity when exposed to varying concentrations of NaCl, H2O2, and UV light. Finally, the environmental impact of the method was evaluated using several assessment tools, including AGREE, ComplexMoGAPI, and BAGI, highlighting its eco-friendly profile.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126559.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glaucoma and its treatment: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.A. Lee, E.J. Higginbotham, Glaucoma and its treatment: a review, American journal of health-system pharmacy, 62 (2005) 691-699, DOI: 10.1093/ajhp/62.7.691.
H. Diekmann, D. Fischer, Glaucoma and optic nerve repair, Cell and tissue research, 353 (2013) 327-337, DOI: 10.1007/s00441-013-1596-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: what is the global burden of visual impairment?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Dandona, R. Dandona, what is the global burden of visual impairment? BMC medicine, 4 (2006) 1-10, DOI: 10.1186/1741-7015-4-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of prostaglandins on the aqueous humor outflow pathways

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.N. Weinreb, C.B. Toris, T.G. B'Ann, J.D. Lindsey, P.L. Kaufman, Effects of prostaglandins on the aqueous humor outflow pathways, Survey of ophthalmology, 47 (2002) S53-S64, DOI: 10.1016/S0039-6257(02)00306-5.

Markowicz-Piasecka, Trends in development and quality assessment of pharmaceutical formulations-F2α analogues in the glaucoma treatment, European Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences, 180 (2023) 106315, DOI: 10.1016/j.ejps.2022.106315.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of prostaglandin analogs in the management of patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kennedy, Pharmacology of natural prostaglandins and analogues, Prostaglandins and Thromboxanes: Butterworths Monographs in Chemistry, (2016) 19.
